comments:
Summary:
This paper addresses online learning and scalable stochastic optimization for non-decomposable performance measures (e.g. Prec@k, partial AUC, F-measure). The authors propose a principled definition of instantaneous penalties for non-decomposable losses, present an FTRL-style online algorithm with sublinear regret under a stability/Lipschitz condition, prove this condition for structural SVM surrogates of Prec@k and pAUC using a novel structural lemma on sorted inner products, and develop two mini-batch stochastic gradient methods (1PMB, 2PMB) with provable convergence to the empirical risk minimizer via uniform convergence results. Experiments on real datasets compare favorably (much faster with competitive accuracy) to cutting-plane and batch baselines.

Strengths:
- The paper tackles an important and under-studied problem: principled online treatment and scalable optimization of non-decomposable losses.
- The instantaneous penalty definition (difference of batch losses) is natural and justified by desiderata; it cleanly generalizes pointwise loss online learning.
- The structural Lemma (Lipschitzness of fixed-position elements in sorted lists of inner products) is a useful technical contribution that enables stability analysis for many ranking-like surrogates.
- The work combines theory and practice: regret bounds for FTRL, uniform convergence proofs for surrogates, and practical stochastic mini-batch algorithms with experiments showing large speedups.
- The 2PMB idea (store rare-class examples and stream the common class) is practical for imbalanced problems like pAUC.

Weaknesses / Concerns:
- Several proofs/results are sketched or deferred to the full version (e.g., some pAUC proofs and details). While sketches are plausible, full proofs are necessary for full verification.
- Some regret bounds for pAUC depend explicitly on T+ and T- and can be large when one class is tiny; this is noted and intuitive, but further discussion of implications and potential mitigations would help.
- Experiments are promising but limited in scope: more datasets, ablation on buffer sizes, sensitivity to hyperparameters, and comparisons to additional recent baselines (if any) would strengthen the empirical claims. Implementation details (exact parameter settings, passes, CPU specs) could be more explicit.
- The paper is dense and technically heavy; reading is made harder by minor typographical issues and repeated lines in the provided text. A bit more exposition intuition around main lemmas would improve accessibility.

Detailed comments / suggestions:
1. Please include the full proofs (or a complete appendix) in the final version to allow readers to verify all technical claims, especially those deferred to a future full version.
2. Expand the experiments: report training time vs accuracy curves, wall-clock per-epoch costs, and results across more datasets and baselines. Ablations on buffer size, epoch length and number of passes would be valuable.
3. Discuss practical choices for surrogate losses and implications when surrogates are non-convex. The current framework assumes convex surrogates; explain limits or possible extensions.
4. Consider whether lower bounds (or impossibility results) exist for regret in this setting to contextualize tightness of the provided bounds.
5. Improve clarity in some technical sections (more intuition around Lemma 2 and how it drives the stability bounds).

Conclusion:
This is a solid, well-motivated contribution that advances both theoretical understanding and practical optimization for non-decomposable losses. The technical novelty (structural lemma, uniform convergence for ranking-like surrogates) and the scalable stochastic methods make it of interest to the learning community. With the addition of the full technical proofs and expanded experimental validation, the work would be very strong. I recommend acceptance.
